assertion.title: Ternary compound operators reduce total nodes 221→208
assertion.statement: Adding 5 ternary operators that fold common compound binary-of-binary patterns into single operations saves 13 nodes across 9 equations, reducing total nodes from 221 to 208 while maintaining 50/50 exact matches.

details.hypothesis: Many expressions use a pattern like binary_op(binary_op(a,b), c) requiring 5 nodes, or more complex compounds like mul(X, cosrule(div(Y,X), Z)) requiring 7 nodes. Folding these into ternary operators saves 1-3 nodes per equation by eliminating the intermediate binary operation.
changes: Added simplification rules: mul(X, cosrule(div(Y,X), Z))→cosrule3, arcsin(div(mulsin(X,Y),Z))→snell3, mul(sqover(X,Z), sin2(Y))→projrange, divsq(mul(X,Y), Z)→mdivsq, sqrtdiv(mul(X,Y), Z)→msqrtdiv
Updated permutation templates and physics templates to use compact ternary forms
details.key_insight: The general principle is that any expression tree of the form op1(op2(a, b), c) can be compressed from 5 nodes to 4 by defining a ternary operator op3(a,b,c) = op1(op2(a,b),c). This is especially powerful for physics patterns where specific compound forms recur: cosine rule needs mul+cosrule+div (7 nodes → 4), Snell's law needs arcsin+div+mulsin (6 → 4), and the common product-then-divide-by-square pattern (divsq(mul(a,b),c)) appears in 3 equations.
